sentences.text: Rachael Rubin.
R-U-B-I-N.
Schmutter.
S-C-H-M-U-T-T-E-R.
1920, October the 4th.
Korzec.
Poland.
Chuna and Pesa.
One sister.
Luba.
He had a store.
But I don't remember my father.
He died when I was small.
A year and a half or two years, approximately.
I don't remember.
No.
No.
My mother was a widow.
And later, I understood because she was always sad.
And then we lived with our grandmother.
Yeah.
Yeah.
My grandmother had a restaurant.
So my mother used to help my grandmother.
And she had other business with cor-- with corn, what she used to buy and sell from the p-- peasant?
Peasant?
Yeah.
And we-- she made a living for us.
Mostly, she was busy, so my grandmother used to take care on us.
She used to--
Ita Leah.
Oh.
She was very nice till, till the end.
She was-- with young people, she was young.
With older, she was old.
And then practical, really, just naturally.
And she was taking care on the house and everything, and the restaurant, too.
She helped a lot.
No.
But she was a practical type.
She was-- she knew everything in the garden.
We had a big garden.
So she used to raise-- we had everything there.
And she used to take care in the garden.
No.
I was too small at the time.
But she was very nice.
She was more than like a mother.
You know, used to bake and cook and you know-- very practical, very-- and we lived together for so many years, till the end of the war-- I mean, till the German came.
And she died before Pesach, before the slaughter.
I went to school, actually.
In the vacations I used to be occupied with all kind craft things, you know, anything, crochet, sewing things, you know.
In the vacation, everything, the libraries.
Mysteries.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
And that was--
Public school, yes.
Polish public school.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was good in school, no problems.
But I, I liked the Hebrew school better.
Some of my friends went to Hebrew school.
But Hebrew school, it was expensive.
We had to pay more.
And my mother couldn't afford.
So I, I finished public school in 1934, I think, '34, '35.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was a mixed population, was Polish and Ukraine, mostly.
So I don't remember how many.
It wasn't a big town.
It was a small.
At that time, yes, when I was young.
We had neighbors, very good neighbors.
Yes.
Yeah.
It was a mixture, you know.
We didn't know the difference at that time.
And they didn't know the difference, that we are Jews in there, those years when I went to school.
But later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later the best neighbors, they became enemies, you know, just during the war.
Walking distance.
I mean, at that time, we walked-- 10, 15 minutes, 20 minutes walk.
Yes.
Sometimes with my sister.
She, she was-- she was in older grades, you know?
Sometimes it was-- I don't remember exactly.
Sad because my father wasn't around.
My grandfather wasn't around.
And during the holiday, especially, you see the lack of, of males in the family, especially during the holidays.
So it was very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother had a hard life, you know, by working and supporting us.
Friday night, it was-- actually, Friday afternoon we finished because the front room, it was the restaurant.
So Friday afternoon, we closed up the doors.
My grandmother used to do it.
And because nobody should come in.
It was finished for the Shabbat.
Lighting candles, having supper.
My grandmother used to bake and cook before for Shabbos.
And then Saturday morning, we didn't light, like an oven.
That she took out from ready-- it was ready warm.
It's a big stove.
It's not like today.
And she took out ready the milk, the warm milk, and everything, and cookies and challah.
So we had our breakfast.
And we went to shul with my grandmother.
And then after lunch, you know, usually, they took a rest, my grandmother and my mother.
And we went to play outside.
I don't remember games.
We were talking.
We were, you know-- we didn't have, ga-- like, toys or-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
When I was sick, I remember, so my mother gave me her jewelry to play in the bath with her jewelry with the chain.
Yeah.
That's what our toys were.
No.
No.
Wintertime, I remember, we were sl-- it was like a hill-- when the snow was-- you know, wintertime.
So we had to slide down and then to walk up and to slide again.
That was our enjoyment.
Summertime, we did our-- yeah, we had a river between our pl-- place where we lived and the middle of the town, like the center of the town.
We had the river.
And it was a par-- a park with trees there.
So we used to go swimming there.
That's what the enjoyment was.
Go to school to learn.
Because when I finished the public school, I was anxious to continue.
But it wasn't possible.
My mother didn't have the money.
And I tried, you know-- a lady came, I remember, and I remind myself, a lady came because I went to ORT to the city of Rovno.
And I was there for a short time.
And my mother called me back because she had to pay too much money for the school and room and-- so I came back.
So she find out that a lady came.
And she was teaching sewing.
So I went to take a course, too, with her, designing, like, to make patterns.
So I went there.
And I was doing this.
T-- she, she taught me how to do this.
Did you enjoy the sewing?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Something to do.
Something-- I'm not used-- I guess from young age, I wasn't used to sit and not to, to do something.
And I enjoyed this because it gave satisfaction when I see something made myself.
I can't remind myself.
I remember my sister was treated better than I was, for instance, because she was the older one.
So before Yom Tov, she used to get new clothes.
For me, my mother used to bring the clothes, the old one, to the dressmaker, and to remodel these on the other side.
You know, to get for me-- but something new, it should be for Yom Tov.
And patent leather shoes, I was always looking at them just-- you know, I was-- I didn't want to wear them often.
They shouldn't get dirty.
[INAUDIBLE]
My cousins.
I had cousins, yes.
And then-- and the cousins, we were close.
I mean, they used to come to my grandmother because they were grandchildren.
So they used to come to my grandmother.
Oh, it was in '38 or at the start of '37, '38.
It started to-- it wasn't the same, you know?
What changed, they used to say that in the government, in the Polish government, German people came in, like, like spies, you know?
Even the Polish government didn't know it of them.
So and they started to rule already against Jews, not to buy in the stores, not to-- how to say-- slaughter them.
It was a shochet, for instance.
They, they said that they shouldn't work, shouldn't kill the chicken the way Jews supposed to, you know?
And we felt already that it's, it's not the same.
Even in school, you know, it's-- they looked at us differently [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] till '39, 1939.
I noticed mostly the-- when, when they came, when, when the war started in Poland.
Then, we noticed.
Yeah.
In the business.
My mother was suffering because, for instance, we had to pay-- what's it-- like income tax, like business tax.
We had a business.
Then they looked, for instance, on the cleanness, and the restaurant should be perfectly, perfectly clean.
Even it was perfect clean, they was-- they were trying to find something, you know.
Said it's not correct.
It's this and this, you know?
It was trouble.
So we tried.
We tried to-- especially my sister was that time helping in the restaurant.
We tried in the evening to wash everything, to make clean, you know, because we knew that maybe they will come in, the inspector, health inspector, like.
And he ca-- when he'll come in, he shouldn't find something wrong.
So and he paid special attention more on us because it was in the same street another restaurant.
They didn't pay as much attention there as they paid on us, you know?
And my mother-- because we're Jews.
And, and my mother was suffering from this, for instance, to pay the taxes in time.
So and sometimes she didn't have the money.
So she-- I mean, she was really young.
She got gray from all the problems what she had.
What did they do?
They, they could like-- we had to pay.
Penalty, like, a fine.
That's right.
We had to pay.
No.
No.
No.
They were so attached-- especially my grandmother was so attached to her garden and to her house, you know?
That never were thinking about moving.
Oh, no.
My mother had chances to go like because she was a widow, and she was still young.
So they matched her up with people, you know, to go to the United States, or-- But she, she wasn't thinking about leaving her mother, my grandmother.
And she didn't want us to have a stepfather.
So we were always together.
Yeah.
When the war started.
And Poland was divided because Poland-- the war in Poland wasn't long, just a few weeks, you know?
So and Poland was divided.
And by us were Russian people.
And the other part of Poland were the German.
A lot of people from Poland came to us because they suffered already from the Germans.
They came, and they warned us.
Said-- they said-- they said what's going on in Poland, you know?
There are killings.
There are all kind of things.
But a lot of people from our part, they left to Russia.
But we were too much attached with our mother and grandmother that we didn't want to leave them, and they didn't want to go.
So we, we stood together.
Then what happened?
It was 18 months the Russians were by us after German started the war with Russia.
I can't say too bad.
OK.
Later.
I, I was working.
I went to school, back, in the evenings to take courses.
And--
Russian, naturally.
During the day, I was working in an office by the Russian.
And I was-- I knew a little bit typing and accounting.
They taught me, actually.
I wasn't a professional accountant, but they taught me how to do this.
And I caught it very easy.
I was working for them.
But it didn't last long.
It's 18 months.
And then they left.
I had a lot of chances to go away.
They begged me, friends and neighbors, people from Poland, when they were new, newcomers, like by us.
They left right away, and they said to me, come.
Don't stay here.
But me with my sister didn't want leave.
So we stood with my mother and grandmother.
Oh, yeah.
I remember the day.
They came, and they made a lot of planes came over our city.
And they started to bomb, throwing bombs.
We ran into the garden-- me, with my mother and sister and grandmother.
And we were under the trees, you know.
A lot of bombs were flying and pieces of bombs.
They were-- it was throwing on us.
They hurt, you know?
For a day, we were covered like that.
And then when it was quiet, already, so we came out.
And we saw a lot of people dead-- neighbors-- because they were running, you know?
They didn't know what to do, and where to cover themselves.
So a lot of people, neighbors, were dead, killed.
These were Jewish neighbors as well as Gentile?
Jewish.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I don't remember exactly, but I remember neighbors, Jewish neighbors, were killed at the time.
And that the day-- the first day when they came, they came in.
They were afraid to go into a city, to a town, until they know that it's clean, that there is no-- I mean, against them.
So they made it clean with the bombs.
No, no.
No damage.
But the garden was-- the earth was upside down, you know, because from the pieces of the bomb.
And then when they came in, it was awful.
Every single day it was-- they collected some people.
They send away to work.
And the people didn't come back.
I at the time was working.
They make like a Judenrat.
You know what it means?
Like a office.
And I was there working on a typewriter, too.
So the-- once--
Why was I chosen?
Because I knew how to work in a office.
So the man, what-- he was the manager, you know, called me I should work for him.
So I was working.
And once a Ukraine guy from the police-- because the Ukraine were right away the managers.
They were the, the-- in the police and the officers.
And so he came in and, he said that he needs to change a tape in the typewriter.
You know, the ta-- it's a old-fashioned typewriter with the black tape and two rolls.
He has to change the ty-- the tape.
I should come there and change the tape.
So the manager says, OK.
Go.
That's it.
So I went.
And it was in the police, police station.
I went.
And I was waiting, waiting.
He didn't give me a tape.
He didn't give me a machine to do this.
So I was asking, what should I do here?
He said come.
I'll show you what to do.
And he brought me in into a toilet.
I should clean the toilet.
We'll go to break now.
